sentences.text: Arthur P.
Stern.
Arthur Stern.
Avraham Benjamin
July 20th, 1925.
Budapest.
Hungary.
My father was Leo Stern, and my mother was Bertha, nee Frankfurter.
I have one brother, Robert.
August 12, 1928.
Very large family, yes.
On both sides.
Oh, yeah.
My family was, on both sides, very Orthodox.
Yes.
In-- in elementary school, I went to the elementary school of the Budapest Orthodox Jewish community.
And then in the next, what you would call junior high, I did the same thing.
And thereafter, problems arose because Orthodox Jews were not supposed to go beyond that.

So I first went to Yeshiva, and I did my intermediate school studies, the so called gymnasium studies, on a private basis, which meant that I didn't go to a public school but I made my state examinations each year.
Was your family very involved in the Jewish community?
Yes.
My father was one of the seven directors of the Budapest Orthodox Jewish community, in charge of economic affairs and finances.
He was an industrialist.
His main businesses were lumber, various companies and various aspects of the lumber business.
He produced lumber.
He [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wholesaled-- manufactured it, he wholesaled it.
He also had retail outlets.
And he was also in the textile business.
No.
No.
I never worked in-- in his enterprises.
Generally speaking, not too much.
We were fairly isolated, particularly the Orthodox community was fairly isolated.
And we were brought up that way.
On the one hand, it was by choice.
On the other hand, of course, as the anti-Jewish movement gained strength, we were isolated by the majority.
Actually before I was born.
Hungary was the first country in Europe which had a so-called numerus clausus, a limited number of admissions to the universities and higher schools, the higher schools of education.
There was a-- a latent antisemitism in the country.
And I think latent is the wrong term.
There was an unofficial antisemitism for ages.
But after World War I, it became official and was expressed in many ways.
Oh, yes.
Well, you went on the streets and some people jeered and called you names.
Oh, Jewish pig.
That sort of thing.
But that was not done all the time.
It happened from time to time.
I think I grew up with it.
I don't remember.
I don't remember having been shocked by it.
It was part of life.
Well, there were many good parts to life.
It's-- my-- my family was very well off, so we could afford just about everything.
There were vacations.
There were the usual things in life.
You could get anything you wanted to.
You just had to ask for it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Family life was very intense, and very loving and very complimentary in a personal sense.
So it was-- it was a great life.
They were mostly Jews.
As a matter of fact, generally, Orthodox Jews.
I-- I hardly got out of the Orthodox Jewish circle during my first, let's say, 17 years.
Then I suddenly start to get in contact with non-Orthodox Jews.
I recall I liked to play soccer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We played with buttons.
We-- we glued buttons together and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of cut them off short, so that a heavy aggregation of buttons could lift a small button.
And the small button was the soccer ball, and the big buttons were the players of soccer.
And you could do that on the window sill in school.
No, in-- in the intermission.
Yes.
It was substantial [INAUDIBLE]
Yes.
Well, that was actually a fairly complicated thing.

My dad and I always wanted me to go to the university, which was very difficult in Hungary because of what started out in the 1920s as a so-called numerus clausus, a limited number of Jews, [INAUDIBLE], a limited number of Jews at the institutes of higher education.
And then gradually, turned into what was referred to, casually, as a numerus [NON-ENGLISH] that the number became close to zero.
Starting 1938, '39, it was almost impossible to get to the universities if you were a Jew.
But people with influence could manage.
And so my [? dad and I ?] in 1943, when I would graduate from high school, I would-- I would go to university.

But the protector of-- the protector of my university education, a gentleman named Bela Kenez, who was a former Minister of the Interior of Hungary, talked to my father about a year before my graduation from the gymnasium and said, if you really want me to get Arthur into the university, he better become a public student, rather than a private student as I was in the past.
Because as a private student, even I couldn't help him get in.
So--
He was on the Board of Directors of several of my father's enterprises.

And so my father reluctantly decided that I should go to the gymnasium of the-- I should become a public student at the gymnasium of the best Jewish community, which was different from the Budapest Orthodox Jewish community.
The Orthodox Jewish community was much smaller and encompassed all of Budapest.
The Jewish community was a-- segmented into three.
There was a best Jewish community, there was a [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] Jewish community.
The best Jewish community was the largest one.
[INAUDIBLE] gymnasium, which was one of the better schools in the country.
And so I-- I entered that in the last year before graduation.
And-- and that, of course, brought me into contact with a lot of non-Orthodox Jews because most of my classmates were non-Orthodox.
Of course I was a kid to a large extent, and so, you know, you picked up these pieces of information very gradually.
I do remember the Anschluss of Austria in 1938.
That was a great shock, at least to me, and-- other people may have expected it.
But there were earlier indications.
Vacations, I remember we were in-- on vacation in Austria in the summer of 1934, my parents and my brother and I, and there was a very strong Nazi movement in Austria at the time.
And one day we got the news that Chancellor Dollfuss of Austria was killed by the Nazis.
I was nine years old at the time.
It was a little bit hard for me to understand, but my dad explained what was going on, and I finally did understand.
So you see, as of the age of-- of nine, we already dealt with antisemitism and Nazis and so forth.
But up to 1938, things were fairly peaceful in Hungary.
There was an unofficial-- and in some respects official-- antisemitism.
I mentioned the limitations at the universities.
But it wasn't too bad.
The Jewish students went to Italy.
They went to Austria, as long as Austria was possible.
My-- my uncle studied at the University of Vienna because he couldn't get into a Hungarian university.
Other people went particularly to Italy, they went to France, [INAUDIBLE] all over Europe and they got their degrees and came back and practiced in Hungary-- practiced professions in Hungary.
But in 1938 was the first year that official discriminatory legislation was established in Hungary.
And so we felt that.
And, of course, '38 was a-- was an eventful year.
Austria was annexed by Germany, I believe in March.
And then came the Czechoslovakian crisis.
And I was to be bar mitzvah that year, in 1938.
My actual bar mitzvah was in the summer, but my parents decided to have the event officially celebrated in the fall, after the holidays, because that was socially better.

And so, in view of the Czechoslovakian crisis, they sent my brother and me to Switzerland for the summer, with the idea that we would be coming back and then there would be the bar mitzvah celebration.
Unfortunately, the Czechoslovakian crisis was not solved by-- solved by the end of the summer.
So they decided to leave us in-- in Switzerland.
And we actually stayed in Switzerland with a-- with a short interruption until after the-- the war started.
We stayed at the Orthodox Jewish school, a boarding school, [?
Institut ?] [?
Ascher, ?] which no longer exists but it existed for many years after the war and for many years before the war.
As a matter of fact, my uncle was at [?
Institut ?] [?
Ascher ?] before World War II.
Before World War I, I'm sorry.
So it-- it was a school with some reputation.
A good Jewish school, very Orthodox, satisfied all the religious requirements.
And so that's where we-- that's where we wound up.
We went home for-- I went home in the summer of '39, in the [INAUDIBLE], in May or so of 1939, to make my annual examination so that I would be officially qualified to go to the next grade.
But otherwise we were, from July '38 to September '39.
My brother and I were in-- in [NON-ENGLISH], French part of Switzerland at the [?
Institut ?] [?
Ascher, ?] where we learned French and became fluent in French.
And had a very good time.
Well, first was the-- first they sent us because they were worried about the Czechoslovakian crisis in the summer of 1938.
Then, as the Czechoslovakian crisis didn't settle, they left us there.

Then, after the Czechoslovakian crisis settled, it became very clear that things are not really settled because only part of Czechoslovakia was absorbed by Germany, but there was a danger that the rest would be absorbed.
So my parents said, why don't you stay some longer?
Then, of course, came March, 1939, when the rest of Czechoslovakia was absorbed by Germany.
After that there was a small pause.
They asked me to go home and to make my examination for the year.
And as soon as I made my examination, my parents decided that the Polish crisis was pretty bad, so they sent us back to Switzerland.
And then you may be-- you may ask the question as to, well, why didn't we stay in Switzerland during the war?
Well, that was very simple because my dad, who-- and I say this seriously-- he was really a leader of men and a very thoughtful individual and very influential individual.
He got together with some other parents who have sent their Jewish children to Switzerland, in a manner similar to the way he and my mother did.

And after World War II broke out in September, they decided that Hungary will, in all likelihood, stay out of the war, but that Switzerland would be crushed between Germany and Italy, and that, therefore, their kids would be better off to return to Hungary.

And so about a dozen of us-- various families unrelated to each other-- were gathered together and we started the trek in late September, 1939, via Italy and Yugoslavia-- you couldn't go through Germany anymore-- back to Hungary.
And that was an interesting trip, but I won't go into the details.
So that's the way we got back Hungary in late September, 1939.
Well, the answer is yes and no.
I was born in 1925.
I think I was fairly mature for my age, And my dad did confide in me fairly early.
But I wasn't home much at the time.
I went to Yeshiva in 19-- see, immediately after my return from Switzerland.
In late 1939, I went to Yeshiva and from '39 to-- '39 to early '42, I spent most of my time in Yeshiva, in [INAUDIBLE] and in [INAUDIBLE].
And the rest of the time when I was home, I tried always to cram in the year's studies, the year's secular studies.
In typically six weeks to get-- get through my examination, my state examination, to be qualified for a higher grade.
And then we went on vacation, and-- so I really wasn't aware of much in-- in-- in Jewish life, in Jewish crisis life, until about March 1942.
That's when I was almost 17 years old.
Yeah, there were-- there were some terrible things that happened.

In Hungary, there was, in 1941, the Jews who were not Hungarian citizens were gathered together, were expelled, and put into Kamianets-Podilskyi, which was Ukraine, Poland, depending on which year you looked at it, and were killed there.
And the rumor of that became known.
When the Yugoslavian crisis broke out in April, 1941, when the attack of the Germans on Yugoslavia occurred, with the help of the Hungarians-- the Hungarian government participated in that.
It's actually a complicated story.
The Hungarian prime minister committed suicide because he didn't want to participate.
The next prime minister, [INAUDIBLE], she did participate.
There were atrocities committed by the Hungarian troops against the Jews in [INAUDIBLE].
And several hundred Jews were killed.
We heard about that.
But in Hungary proper, there was no killing.
In Hungary proper, there was a systematic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anti-Jewish legislation which deprived much of the Jewish population of their livelihood.
And--
It didn't really affect my father.
My father was heading a variety of major corporations, and, as it happens very frequently, the rich people became richer, and the poor people became poorer.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I heard about these things.
I remember a conversation I had about the deportation in-- the deportation of the non-Hungarian citizen Jews with my father.
And my father, of course, was himself quite concerned, because his family was naturalized Hungarian citizens.
But after that-- after a while, that went away.
I recall a conversation I had with one of my so-called classmates.
We-- there were a few of us who didn't go to school but who weren't public students but were private students.
And we met at a given day in the year to pass our examinations together.
There five, six, seven of us who did that together.
And one of the ones who was with me in, I believe, 1942, was one of the survivors of the [INAUDIBLE] Massacre.
And so I learned about that going on.

And, of course, one knew about-- about the anti-Jewish legislation, about the fact that the majority of the Jewish population was unemployed, because the employment rules were such that Jews could be employed only in limited numbers.
So particularly the white collar and educated Jews became unemployed.
You heard about that.
There were supporting activities.

And, of course, in our case, my father was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chairman of the Orthodox Rescue Committee, which was called [NON-ENGLISH] in Budapest, which was available to receive Jewish refugees from the surrounding countries.
That is, no longer Austria-- that was long over by that time-- but Slovakia, the occupied portions of [INAUDIBLE] and Soviet Union, Romania, and Yugoslavia.
Hungary became a haven for Jewish refugees.
The Hungarian government was not bloody.
The Hungarian government was after money, and they deprived Jews of their livelihood to a large extent.
They ostensibly cooperated with the Germans, and they did cooperate with the Germans.
But in Jewish matters, they refused to be bloody.
So thousands and thousands of Jewish refugees congregated in Hungary, and my father was the Orthodox head-- was the head of the Orthodox organization, which supported these people.
I was away from home much of the time, but as of March or April, 1942, I was at home.
And so I saw the stream of these people coming, and I gradually became quite involved.
And my dad sort of used me as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] partly his errand boy, partly his aide in a variety of activities that were needed to support these people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as time went on, after 1942, I became increasingly aware of what was going on.
And we had refugees who told us about what was going on in Poland.
And these were refugees from a variety of camps.
Some of them were-- were non-Jewish refugees.

My father was a very close friend of a refugee Polish general who was, of course, not Jewish-- there were no Jewish generals in Poland-- but who maintained contact with Poland and who reported on what was going on.
So I guess to summarize it, from-- [INAUDIBLE], these were parallel developments.
I grew up from being a kid to becoming a young man to understand these things.
And the information was coming in, and I gradually assimilated the information.
I'm not sure whether I'm answering your question, but by-- I would say by the time that I graduated from the gymnasium in June, 1943, I think that I was very informed on what was going on.
And what was your sense of that?
Well, on the one hand, of course, it was a sense of horror and lack of understanding, and, at the beginning, lack of belief.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Quite a few of us were not quite as close to activities as I was-- and I was only because of my father-- but most Hungarian Jews simply rejected the information.
They simply didn't believe it.
They felt that such a thing is impossible in the 20th century.
And this belief lasted until beyond the German-- German occupation.
People went to their deaths without believing that they are going to their death.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So that was-- there was a degree of non-belief, a degree of-- of, well, it couldn't happen here.
It was quite irrational.
It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's difficult to understand today.
It's difficult for me to understand that it was not clear to us that the same thing will sooner or later happen to us.

I don't know how it is possible that we believed that Hitler is just going to let little Hungary play its games, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ostensibly cooperating with him, but really not suppressing their Jews.
But that's what people hoped for.
My dad had a visa for Palestine in 1939, which he didn't use.
I got a certificate to Palestine in 1943 from Dr.
Kasztner.
Who had a few available.
And-- and I didn't use it, because my dad objected.
He didn't-- he didn't want his son to go too-- too-- too far away.
He wanted to keep his son around.
Rezso Kasztner-- Dr.
Kasztner was, in effect, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the two or three real heads of the Zionist movement in Hungary.
He-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's a complicated story, because I say Hungary, and the boundaries of Hungary changed almost year after year.
In 1940, Hitler made a decision to return to Hungary, from Romania, the northern part of Transylvania, which was lost by Hungary to Romania in 1918-- 1920, really, officially.
And with that, a large number of Romanian Jews suddenly became Hungarian Jews.
Rezso Kasztner belonged to that category of people.
He came from Cluj [INAUDIBLE], which was the capital of that particular area.
And--
Well, that's a complicated story too.
The people who started rescue activities in Hungary for refugee Jews were two groups of people.
The Zionists on the one hand, who were, generally speaking, non-religious, and who started their own [NON-ENGLISH], their own committee of-- of help and rescue.
And independently of that, there was an Orthodox [NON-ENGLISH].

These two groups, the Zionists on the one hand and the Orthodox on the other hand, represented a minority of the Jews in Budapest, and represented even a minority of the Jews in Hungary, although in the countryside, the Orthodox were quite numerous.
